content.text: A force is a push or pull applied on an object. In everyday life, we experience forces constantly—pushing or pulling objects in various ways. The SI unit of force is the newton, written as 'N' (named after Isaac Newton).
Forces are always the result of interaction between two objects. When you push a table, your hand (one object) and the table (another object) interact, and a force is generated.

sections.section_title: 5.2 What Can a Force Do to the Bodies on Which It Is Applied?

content.text: The application of force on an object can produce several effects. A single force can cause one or multiple effects simultaneously.

items: Make an object move from rest—applying a push or pull can initiate motion of a stationary object
Change the speed of a moving object—force can increase speed (acceleration) or decrease speed (deceleration)
Change the direction of motion—force can alter the path that an object is following without changing its speed
Bring about a change in shape—force can deform or change the structure of an object
Cause a combination of these effects—force can simultaneously change speed and direction

content.text: Examples: A fielder stopping a moving ball applies force to decrease its speed; hitting a ball with a bat changes its direction; pressing a balloon deforms it; opening a door requires force to change its position.

If an object is at rest despite forces acting on it, the forces are balanced—they cancel each other out. An object in motion will continue moving at constant velocity only if no net force acts on it (Newton's First Law, to be learned in higher grades).

sections.section_title: 5.3 Are Forces an Interaction Between Two or More Objects?

content.text: Forces are always the result of interactions between two or more objects. At minimum, two objects must interact for a force to come into play. When you push a table, your hand and the table are two objects interacting.

Importantly, whenever two objects interact, each object experiences a force from the other. When you push a table, your hand experiences a reaction force from the table. As soon as the interaction stops, both forces disappear.

In all activities involving force—pushing a door, lifting an object, pulling a rope, catching a ball—at least two objects are always involved in the interaction. The force experienced by one object is exerted by the other object, and vice versa.

sections.section_title: 5.4 What Are the Different Types of Forces?

content.text: Contact forces are forces that require physical contact between two objects. The force acts only when the objects touch each other. Contact can be direct (using hands or body parts) or indirect (using tools like sticks or ropes).
Two main types of contact forces are muscular force and friction.
Muscular force is the force resulting from the action of muscles in the body. When muscles contract and elongate (stretch), they produce force that enables movement and various physical activities.

items: Humans use muscular force for walking, running, lifting, pushing, jumping, stretching, and many other physical activities
Animals, birds, fish, and insects use muscular forces for movement and survival
Humans historically used the muscular force of animals (horses, oxen, elephants) to perform tasks like transportation and agriculture

content.text: Muscular force plays crucial internal roles as well: it helps us chew and swallow food, moves food through the digestive tract, and enables the heart to pump blood throughout the body—all essential for survival.

Friction is a contact force that arises when one object moves (or tries to move) over another surface. When you push an object on a table, it eventually stops even though you no longer apply force—this is due to friction.

The force of friction is the force that comes into play when an object moves or tries to move over another surface. Friction always acts in a direction opposite to the direction of motion (or attempted motion) of the object.

Causes of friction: Friction arises due to irregularities (roughness) in the surfaces in contact. Even surfaces that appear smooth have minute irregularities. When two surfaces are placed in contact, the irregularities lock into each other and create resistance to motion.

Factors affecting friction: The amount of friction depends on the nature of the surfaces in contact. Rough surfaces produce greater friction than smooth surfaces. When testing the same object on different surfaces (glass, cloth, wood, ceramic, sand), it stops after travelling different distances.

Friction acts not only on solid surfaces but also on objects moving through liquids (water) and gases (air). This is why aeroplanes, ships, boats, and high-speed trains are designed with streamlined shapes to reduce friction from air and water.
Non-contact forces are forces that can act on objects even when they are not in physical contact with each other. The effect of these forces can be experienced across a distance.
Three main types of non-contact forces are magnetic force, electrostatic force, and gravitational force.
A magnet can exert force on another magnet or on magnetic materials without being in contact with them. This demonstrates that magnetic force acts from a distance.
Magnetic force rules: Like poles (North-North or South-South) repel each other, while unlike poles (North-South) attract each other. A magnet attracts objects made of magnetic materials like iron.
Electromagnets (coils of wire carrying electric current) also behave like magnets and can exert magnetic forces on other magnets and magnetic materials from a distance.

When two objects made of certain materials are rubbed together, electrical charges build up on their surfaces. These stationary charges are called static charges. A charged object can exert force on uncharged or charged objects even without contact.
In Activity 5.6: When a plastic scale is rubbed with polythene, it becomes charged. When brought near small pieces of paper, the charged scale attracts the paper pieces without touching them.

In Activity 5.7: When two balloons are rubbed with a woollen cloth, both become similarly charged. When released, the two similarly charged balloons repel each other. When the woollen cloth (oppositely charged) is brought near a balloon, they attract each other.

Types of electric charges: There are two kinds of static charges—positive and negative. Similarly charged objects (like charges) repel each other. Objects with opposite charges (unlike charges) attract each other.
When charges move through a circuit, they constitute an electric current. This same current creates the heating and magnetic effects discussed in previous chapters.

The gravitational force is the force with which the Earth attracts all objects towards itself. It is also called force of gravity or simply gravity. When you throw a ball upwards, it slows down, stops momentarily, and falls back down due to gravitational force.

Characteristics of gravitational force: Gravitational force acts without contact with the object—it is a non-contact force. Unlike magnetic and electrostatic forces (which can be attractive or repulsive), gravitational force is always attractive. It pulls objects towards the Earth, never repels them.

Effects on motion: Objects falling downward experience downward gravitational force, causing their speed to increase. Objects thrown upward initially move upward against gravitational force, which acts downward, causing their speed to decrease. At the topmost point, the velocity becomes zero momentarily before the object begins falling.

The weight of an object is the force with which the Earth pulls that object towards itself. Weight is a measure of how strongly the Earth's gravitational force acts on the object. Since weight is a force, it is measured in the SI unit of newton (N).

Weight varies with objects: Different objects have different weights because the Earth pulls different objects with different forces. A heavier object (more massive) experiences stronger gravitational pull than a lighter object.

Spring balance: A spring balance is a simple device used to measure weight. It consists of a spring fixed at one end with a hook at the other end. When an object is hung from the hook, the spring stretches according to the weight of the object. The scale on the balance is marked to show weight in newtons (N) and sometimes also corresponds mass values in grams (g).

Reading a spring balance: (1) Identify the range of the balance—note the maximum weight it can measure. (2) Determine the smallest division—calculate the weight represented by one small mark on the scale. (3) Hang the object and read the scale carefully. Example: If the distance between two bigger marks represents 1 N and there are 5 smaller divisions between them, each small division = 1N/5 = 0.2 N.
Weight and mass are often confused but are fundamentally different concepts.

items: Mass: The amount of matter in an object. Measured in grams (g) or kilograms (kg). Remains the same everywhere—on Earth, on the Moon, or any other planet.
Weight: The gravitational force with which the Earth (or another planet) pulls an object. Measured in newtons (N). Can vary from place to place on Earth and is very different on different planets.

content.text: Example: A 1 kg object has the same mass on Earth and on the Moon. However, its weight is different: On Earth, its weight is approximately 10 N. On the Moon, the same object's weight is only 1.6 N because the Moon's gravity is much weaker. On Jupiter, it would weigh 25.4 N because Jupiter's gravity is much stronger.
Calculating weight from mass: Weight = Mass × Gravitational acceleration (g), where g ≈ 10 m/s² on Earth.

In everyday language, people often use 'weight' to mean 'mass' (e.g., 'The weight of the bag is 10 kg'), but scientifically, this is incorrect. In scientific context, it is important to use the correct terms with their correct units.

When an object is placed in a liquid, the liquid applies an upward force on the object. This upward force is called upthrust or buoyant force. When you push a bottle filled with water (lid closed) into a bucket of water, you feel an upward push. When released, the bottle bounces back to the surface.
The buoyant force is applied by the liquid on the object in the upward direction, acting opposite to the gravitational force (which acts downward).
When an object is placed in a liquid, two forces act on it: (1) Gravitational force (weight) acting downward. (2) Buoyant force (upthrust) acting upward.
Three possible outcomes based on the comparison of these forces:

items: If gravitational force > buoyant force: The downward force is greater, so the object sinks to the bottom of the liquid.
If gravitational force = buoyant force: The two forces balance each other, so the object floats in the liquid at the surface or suspended within it.
If gravitational force < buoyant force: The upward force is greater, causing the object to rise and float on the surface.

content.text: One of the main factors that affects buoyant force is the density of the liquid. Denser liquids provide stronger buoyant force.

Archimedes, a famous Greek scientist, discovered that when an object is fully or partially immersed in a liquid, it experiences an upward force equal to the weight of the liquid it displaces. This is known as Archimedes' Principle.

Application of Archimedes' Principle: (1) If the weight of liquid displaced by an object is smaller than the weight of the object, the object sinks. (2) If the weight of liquid displaced equals the weight of the object, the object floats.

Pumice is an example of a natural floating rock. It is formed during volcanic eruptions when lava with gas and water vapour cools quickly, trapping tiny air bubbles inside. These air pockets make it less dense than water, allowing it to float.

glossary.definition: A push or pull applied on an object, resulting from interaction with another object. SI unit: newton (N).
A force that requires physical contact between two objects to be applied. Examples: muscular force, friction.
A force that can act on objects even without physical contact between them, working across distances. Examples: magnetic force, gravitational force, electrostatic force.
The force resulting from the contraction and elongation of muscles in living organisms, used for movement and survival.
A contact force that arises when one object moves or tries to move over another surface; acts opposite to the direction of motion.
The force exerted by a magnet on another magnet or magnetic material, acting from a distance; follows the rule that like poles repel and unlike poles attract.
The force exerted by a charged body on another charged or uncharged body, acting from a distance; like charges repel and unlike charges attract.
Electrical charge that builds up on the surface of an object when two objects of certain materials are rubbed together; stationary and does not move by itself.
The force with which the Earth (or any celestial body) attracts objects towards itself; always attractive; acts from a distance.
The gravitational force with which the Earth pulls an object towards itself; measured in newtons (N); varies from place to place and on different planets.
The amount of matter in an object; measured in grams (g) or kilograms (kg); remains constant regardless of location.
A device consisting of a spring and hook used to measure the weight (force) of objects; scale is marked in newtons.
The upward force applied by a liquid on an object placed in it, equal to the weight of liquid displaced by the object.
A principle stating that an object immersed in a liquid experiences an upward force equal to the weight of the liquid it displaces.
The SI unit of force, named after Isaac Newton.

summary_points.description: A force is a push or pull on an object resulting from its interaction with another object. Forces are always generated through interaction between at least two objects. The SI unit of force is the newton (N).

Forces can change an object's motion: making it move from rest, changing its speed (acceleration or deceleration), changing its direction of motion, or changing its shape. A single force can produce multiple effects simultaneously.

summary_points.point: Contact Forces: Muscular Force and Friction
summary_points.description: Contact forces require physical contact between objects. Muscular force results from muscle contraction and is used for movement by all living organisms. Friction arises when one object moves over another surface, acting opposite to the direction of motion, and depends on the roughness of surfaces.

Magnetic force is a non-contact force exerted by magnets on other magnets or magnetic materials from a distance. Like poles repel each other while unlike poles attract. Electromagnets (current-carrying coils) also produce magnetic forces.

Electrostatic force is a non-contact force resulting from static charges on objects. When two materials are rubbed together, they acquire opposite charges. Like charges repel while unlike charges attract. This force can act across distances without contact.

Gravitational force is the force with which Earth attracts all objects towards itself (always attractive, non-contact force). Weight is the gravitational force acting on an object, measured in newtons. Weight varies with location and planet, but mass remains constant.

Mass is the amount of matter in an object (measured in kg or g), constant everywhere. Weight is the gravitational force on an object (measured in N), varying with location and celestial body. On Earth: Weight ≈ Mass × 10.

A spring balance measures weight by the amount it stretches when an object is hung from it. To read a spring balance accurately, identify the range, determine the value of smallest divisions, and read the scale carefully.

When an object is in a liquid, upthrust (buoyant force) acts upward while gravitational force acts downward. If weight > buoyant force, object sinks. If weight = buoyant force, object floats. Archimedes' Principle states that buoyant force equals the weight of displaced liquid.
